<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_4-7-outlier"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--4.7-outlier"></a>

### [4.7 Outlier](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_4-7-outlier)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-31-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-32"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-31)

```sql
create function outlier as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.anomaly.UDTFOutlier'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-31-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-32"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-31)

This function is used to detect distance-based outliers. For each point in the current window, if the number of its neighbors within the distance of neighbor distance threshold is less than the neighbor count threshold, the point in detected as an outlier.

**Name:** OUTLIER

**Input Series：** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

- `r`：the neighbor distance threshold.
- `k`：the neighbor count threshold.
- `w`：the window size.
- `s`：the slide size.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is the same as the input.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-31-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-32"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-31)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--assigning-parameters-of-queries"></a>

##### [Assigning Parameters of Queries](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--assigning-parameters-of-queries)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+------------+
|                         Time|root.test.s1|
+-----------------------------+------------+
|2020-01-04T23:59:55.000+08:00|        56.0|
|2020-01-04T23:59:56.000+08:00|        55.1|
|2020-01-04T23:59:57.000+08:00|        54.2|
|2020-01-04T23:59:58.000+08:00|        56.3|
|2020-01-04T23:59:59.000+08:00|        59.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:00.000+08:00|        60.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:01.000+08:00|        60.5|
|2020-01-05T00:00:02.000+08:00|        64.5|
|2020-01-05T00:00:03.000+08:00|        69.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:04.000+08:00|        64.2|
|2020-01-05T00:00:05.000+08:00|        62.3|
|2020-01-05T00:00:06.000+08:00|        58.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:07.000+08:00|        58.9|
|2020-01-05T00:00:08.000+08:00|        52.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:09.000+08:00|        62.3|
|2020-01-05T00:00:10.000+08:00|        61.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:11.000+08:00|        64.2|
|2020-01-05T00:00:12.000+08:00|        61.8|
|2020-01-05T00:00:13.000+08:00|        64.0|
|2020-01-05T00:00:14.000+08:00|        63.0|
+-----------------------------+------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select outlier(s1,"r"="5.0","k"="4","w"="10","s"="5") from root.test
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------+
|                         Time|outlier(root.test.s1,"r"="5.0","k"="4","w"="10","s"="5")|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------+
|2020-01-05T00:00:03.000+08:00|                                                    69.0|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------+
|2020-01-05T00:00:08.000+08:00|                                                    52.0|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-frequency-domain-analysis"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.-frequency-domain-analysis"></a>

## [5. Frequency Domain Analysis](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-frequency-domain-analysis)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-1-conv"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.1-conv"></a>

### [5.1 Conv](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-1-conv)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-32-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-33"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-32)

```sql
create function conv as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFConv'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-32-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-33"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-32)

This function is used to calculate the convolution, i.e. polynomial multiplication.

**Name:** CONV

**Input:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. It is the result of convolution whose timestamps starting from 0 only indicate the order.

**Note:** `NaN` in the input series will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-32-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-33"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-32)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d2.s1|root.test.d2.s2|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|            1.0|            7.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|            0.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|            1.0|           null|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select conv(s1,s2) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+ |                         Time|conv(root.test.d2.s1, root.test.d2.s2)| +-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                   7.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|                                   2.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|                                   7.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|                                   2.0| +-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-2-deconv"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.2-deconv"></a>

### [5.2 Deconv](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-2-deconv)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-33-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-34"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-33)

```sql
create function deconv as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFDeconv'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-33-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-34"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-33)

This function is used to calculate the deconvolution, i.e. polynomial division.

**Name:** DECONV

**Input:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `result`: The result of deconvolution, which is 'quotient' or 'remainder'. By default, the quotient will be output.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. It is the result of deconvolving the second series from the first series (dividing the first series by the second series) whose timestamps starting from 0 only indicate the order.

**Note:** `NaN` in the input series will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-33-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-34"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-33)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--calculate-the-quotient"></a>

##### [Calculate the quotient](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--calculate-the-quotient)

When `result` is 'quotient' or the default, this function calculates the quotient of the deconvolution.

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+ |                         Time|root.test.d2.s3|root.test.d2.s2| +-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|            8.0|            7.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|            2.0|            2.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|            7.0|           null| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|            2.0|           null| +-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select deconv(s3,s2) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
|                         Time|deconv(root.test.d2.s3, root.test.d2.s2)|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                     1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|                                     0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|                                     1.0|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--calculate-the-remainder"></a>

##### [Calculate the remainder](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--calculate-the-remainder)

When `result` is 'remainder', this function calculates the remainder of the deconvolution.

Input series is the same as above, the SQL for query is shown below:

```sql
select deconv(s3,s2,'result'='remainder') from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+ |                         Time|deconv(root.test.d2.s3, root.test.d2.s2, "result"="remainder")| +-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                                           1.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|                                                           0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|                                                           0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|                                                           0.0| +-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-3-dwt"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.3-dwt"></a>

### [5.3 DWT](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-3-dwt)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-34-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-35"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-34)

```sql
create function dwt as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFDWT'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-34-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-35"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-34)

This function is used to calculate 1d discrete wavelet transform of a numerical series.

**Name:** DWT

**Input:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `method`: The type of wavelet. May select 'Haar', 'DB4', 'DB6', 'DB8', where DB means Daubechies. User may offer coefficients of wavelet transform and ignore this parameter. Case ignored.
- `coef`: Coefficients of wavelet transform. When providing this parameter, use comma ',' to split them, and leave no spaces or other punctuations.
- `layer`: Times to transform. The number of output vectors equals

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>l</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><annotation>layer+1</annotation></semantics></math>

  layer+1. Default is 1.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. The length is the same as the input.

**Note:** The length of input series must be an integer number power of 2.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-34-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-35"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-34)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--haar-wavelet-transform"></a>

##### [Haar wavelet transform](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--haar-wavelet-transform)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d1.s1|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|            0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.100+08:00|            0.2|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.200+08:00|            1.5|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.300+08:00|            1.2|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.400+08:00|            0.6|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.500+08:00|            1.7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.600+08:00|            0.8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.700+08:00|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.800+08:00|            2.5|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.900+08:00|            2.1|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|            0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.100+08:00|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.200+08:00|            1.8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.300+08:00|            1.2|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.400+08:00|            1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.500+08:00|            1.6|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select dwt(s1,"method"="haar") from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|                         Time|dwt(root.test.d1.s1, "method"="haar")|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                  0.14142135834465192|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.100+08:00|                    1.909188342921157|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.200+08:00|                   1.6263456473052773|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.300+08:00|                   1.9798989957517026|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.400+08:00|                    3.252691126023161|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.500+08:00|                    1.414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.600+08:00|                   2.1213203435596424|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.700+08:00|                   1.8384776479437628|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.800+08:00|                 -0.14142135834465192|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.900+08:00|                  0.21213200063848547|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|                  -0.7778174761639416|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.100+08:00|                  -0.8485281289944873|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.200+08:00|                   0.2828427799095765|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.300+08:00|                   -1.414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.400+08:00|                  0.42426400127697095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.500+08:00|                 -0.42426408557066786|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-4-idwt"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.4-idwt"></a>

### [5.4 IDWT](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-4-idwt)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-35-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-36"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-35)

```sql
create function dwt as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFIDWT'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-35-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-36"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-35)

This function performs one-dimensional inverse discrete wavelet transform on the input series, reconstructing the original data from DWT decomposed wavelet coefficients.

**Name:** IDWT

**Input:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `method`: The type of wavelet. May select 'Haar', 'DB4', 'DB6', 'DB8', where DB means Daubechies. User may offer coefficients of wavelet transform and ignore this parameter. Case ignored.
- `coef`: Coefficients of wavelet transform. When providing this parameter, use comma ',' to split them, and leave no spaces or other punctuations.
- `layer`: Times to transform. The number of output vectors equals

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>l</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><annotation>layer+1</annotation></semantics></math>

  layer+1. Default is 1.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. The length is the same as the input.

**Note:**

- The length of input series must be an integer number power of 2.
- The parameter settings of the IDWT function (method/coef/layer) should be consistent with the corresponding DWT transformation to correctly reconstruct the original data.
- Typically, the input of IDWT is the output result of the DWT function.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-35-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-36"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-35)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--haar-wavelet-transform-1"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--haar-wavelet-transform-2"></a>

##### [Haar wavelet transform](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--haar-wavelet-transform-1)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------+
|                         Time|     root.test.d1.s2|
+-----------------------------+--------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|  0.1414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.100+08:00|   1.909188309203678|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.200+08:00|  1.6263455967290592|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.300+08:00|   1.979898987322333|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.400+08:00|  3.2526911934581184|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.500+08:00|   1.414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.600+08:00|  2.1213203435596424|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.700+08:00|  1.8384776310850235|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.800+08:00| -0.1414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.900+08:00| 0.21213203435596428|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00| -0.7778174593052022|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.100+08:00| -0.8485281374238569|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.200+08:00|  0.2828427124746189|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.300+08:00|  -1.414213562373095|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.400+08:00| 0.42426406871192857|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.500+08:00|-0.42426406871192857|
+-----------------------------+--------------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select idwt(s2,"method"="haar") from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+
|                         Time|idwt(root.test.d1.s2, "method"="haar")|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                   0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.100+08:00|                   0.19999999999999998|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.200+08:00|                    1.4999999999999996|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.300+08:00|                    1.1999999999999997|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.400+08:00|                                   0.6|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.500+08:00|                    1.6999999999999997|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.600+08:00|                    0.7999999999999998|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.700+08:00|                    1.9999999999999996|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.800+08:00|                    2.4999999999999996|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.900+08:00|                                   2.1|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|                                   0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.100+08:00|                    1.9999999999999996|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.200+08:00|                    1.7999999999999998|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.300+08:00|                    1.1999999999999997|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.400+08:00|                    0.9999999999999998|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.500+08:00|                    1.5999999999999999|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-5-fft"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.5-fft"></a>

### [5.5 FFT](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-5-fft)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-36-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-37"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-36)

```sql
create function fft as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFFFT'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-36-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-37"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-36)

This function is used to calculate the fast Fourier transform (FFT) of a numerical series.

**Name:** FFT

**Input:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `method`: The type of FFT, which is 'uniform' (by default) or 'nonuniform'. If the value is 'uniform', the timestamps will be ignored and all data points will be regarded as equidistant. Thus, the equidistant fast Fourier transform algorithm will be applied. If the value is 'nonuniform' (TODO), the non-equidistant fast Fourier transform algorithm will be applied based on timestamps.
- `result`: The result of FFT, which is 'real', 'imag', 'abs' or 'angle', corresponding to the real part, imaginary part, magnitude and phase angle. By default, the magnitude will be output.
- `compress`: The parameter of compression, which is within (0,1]. It is the reserved energy ratio of lossy compression. By default, there is no compression.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. The length is the same as the input. The timestamps starting from 0 only indicate the order.

**Note:** `NaN` in the input series will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-36-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-37"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-36)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--uniform-fft"></a>

##### [Uniform FFT](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--uniform-fft)

With the default `type`, uniform FFT is applied.

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d1.s1|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|       2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:02.000+08:00|     -2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:03.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:04.000+08:00|            1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:05.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:06.000+08:00|      0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:07.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:08.000+08:00|      -0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:09.000+08:00|            0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:10.000+08:00|       0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:11.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:12.000+08:00|     -0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:13.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:14.000+08:00|           -1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:15.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:16.000+08:00|      2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:17.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:18.000+08:00|      -2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:19.000+08:00|            0.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select fft(s1) from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------+
|                         Time|  fft(root.test.d1.s1)|
+-----------------------------+----------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                   0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00| 1.2727111142703152E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|  2.385520799101839E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|  8.723291723972645E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|    19.999999960195904|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|     9.999999850988388|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.006+08:00| 3.2260694930700566E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.007+08:00|  8.723291605373329E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.008+08:00|  1.108657103979944E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.009+08:00| 1.2727110997246171E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.010+08:00|1.9852334701272664E-23|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.011+08:00| 1.2727111194499847E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.012+08:00|  1.108657103979944E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.013+08:00|  8.723291785769131E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.014+08:00|  3.226069493070057E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.015+08:00|     9.999999850988388|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.016+08:00|    19.999999960195904|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.017+08:00|  8.723291747109068E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.018+08:00| 2.3855207991018386E-7|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.019+08:00| 1.2727112069910878E-8|
+-----------------------------+----------------------+
```

Note: The input is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>y=sin(2\pi t/4)+2sin(2\pi t/5)</annotation></semantics></math>

y=sin(2πt/4)+2sin(2πt/5) with a length of 20. Thus, there are peaks in

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow><annotation>k=4</annotation></semantics></math>

k=4 and

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><annotation>k=5</annotation></semantics></math>

k=5 of the output.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--uniform-fft-with-compression"></a>

##### [Uniform FFT with Compression](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--uniform-fft-with-compression)

Input series is the same as above, the SQL for query is shown below:

```sql
select fft(s1, 'result'='real', 'compress'='0.99'), fft(s1, 'result'='imag','compress'='0.99') from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+ |                         Time|  fft(root.test.d1.s1,|  fft(root.test.d1.s1,| |                             |      "result"="real",|      "result"="imag",| |                             |    "compress"="0.99")|    "compress"="0.99")| +-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                   0.0|                   0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00| -3.932894010461041E-9| 1.2104201863039066E-8| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|-1.4021739447490164E-7| 1.9299268669082926E-7| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00| -7.057291240286645E-8|  5.127422242345858E-8| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|    19.021130288047125|    -6.180339875198807| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|     9.999999850988388| 3.501852745067114E-16| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.019+08:00| -3.932894898639461E-9|-1.2104202549376264E-8| +-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
```

Note: Based on the conjugation of the Fourier transform result, only the first half of the compression result is reserved. According to the given parameter, data points are reserved from low frequency to high frequency until the reserved energy ratio exceeds it. The last data point is reserved to indicate the length of the series.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-6-highpass"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.6-highpass"></a>

### [5.6 HighPass](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-6-highpass)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-37-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-38"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-37)

```sql
create function highpass as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFHighPass'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-37-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-38"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-37)

This function performs low-pass filtering on the input series and extracts components above the cutoff frequency. The timestamps of input will be ignored and all data points will be regarded as equidistant.

**Name:** HIGHPASS

**Input:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `wpass`: The normalized cutoff frequency which values (0,1). This parameter cannot be lacked.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. It is the input after filtering. The length and timestamps of output are the same as the input.

**Note:** `NaN` in the input series will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-37-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-38"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-37)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d1.s1|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|       2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:02.000+08:00|     -2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:03.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:04.000+08:00|            1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:05.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:06.000+08:00|      0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:07.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:08.000+08:00|      -0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:09.000+08:00|            0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:10.000+08:00|       0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:11.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:12.000+08:00|     -0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:13.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:14.000+08:00|           -1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:15.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:16.000+08:00|      2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:17.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:18.000+08:00|      -2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:19.000+08:00|            0.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select highpass(s1,'wpass'='0.45') from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
|                         Time|highpass(root.test.d1.s1, "wpass"="0.45")|
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                       0.9999999534830373|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|                    1.7462829277628608E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:02.000+08:00|                      -0.9999999593178128|
|1970-01-01T08:00:03.000+08:00|                   -4.1115269056426626E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:04.000+08:00|                       0.9999999925494194|
|1970-01-01T08:00:05.000+08:00|                     3.328126513330016E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:06.000+08:00|                      -1.0000000183304454|
|1970-01-01T08:00:07.000+08:00|                    6.260191433311374E-10|
|1970-01-01T08:00:08.000+08:00|                       1.0000000018134796|
|1970-01-01T08:00:09.000+08:00|                   -3.097210911744423E-17|
|1970-01-01T08:00:10.000+08:00|                      -1.0000000018134794|
|1970-01-01T08:00:11.000+08:00|                   -6.260191627862097E-10|
|1970-01-01T08:00:12.000+08:00|                       1.0000000183304454|
|1970-01-01T08:00:13.000+08:00|                    -3.328126501424346E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:14.000+08:00|                      -0.9999999925494196|
|1970-01-01T08:00:15.000+08:00|                     4.111526915498874E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:16.000+08:00|                       0.9999999593178128|
|1970-01-01T08:00:17.000+08:00|                   -1.7462829341296528E-8|
|1970-01-01T08:00:18.000+08:00|                      -0.9999999534830369|
|1970-01-01T08:00:19.000+08:00|                   -1.035237222742873E-16|
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
```

Note: The input is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>y=sin(2\pi t/4)+2sin(2\pi t/5)</annotation></semantics></math>

y=sin(2πt/4)+2sin(2πt/5) with a length of 20. Thus, the output is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>y=sin(2\pi t/4)</annotation></semantics></math>

y=sin(2πt/4) after high-pass filtering.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-7-ifft"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.7-ifft"></a>

### [5.7 IFFT](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-7-ifft)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-38-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-39"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-38)

```sql
create function ifft as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFIFFT'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-38-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-39"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-38)

This function treats the two input series as the real and imaginary part of a complex series, performs an inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT), and outputs the real part of the result.
For the input format, please refer to the output format of `FFT` function.
Moreover, the compressed output of `FFT` function is also supported.

**Name:** IFFT

**Input:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `start`: The start time of the output series with the format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'. By default, it is '1970-01-01 08:00:00'.
- `interval`: The interval of the output series, which is a positive number with an unit. The unit is 'ms' for millisecond, 's' for second, 'm' for minute, 'h' for hour and 'd' for day. By default, it is 1s.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. It is strictly equispaced. The values are the results of IFFT.

**Note:** If a row contains null points or `NaN`, it will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-38-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-39"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-38)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+ |                         Time|       root.test.d1.re|       root.test.d1.im| +-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                   0.0|                   0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00| -3.932894010461041E-9| 1.2104201863039066E-8| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|-1.4021739447490164E-7| 1.9299268669082926E-7| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00| -7.057291240286645E-8|  5.127422242345858E-8| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|    19.021130288047125|    -6.180339875198807| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|     9.999999850988388| 3.501852745067114E-16| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.019+08:00| -3.932894898639461E-9|-1.2104202549376264E-8| +-----------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select ifft(re, im, 'interval'='1m', 'start'='2021-01-01 00:00:00') from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                         Time|ifft(root.test.d1.re, root.test.d1.im, "interval"="1m",|
|                             |                         "start"="2021-01-01 00:00:00")|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+
|2021-01-01T00:00:00.000+08:00|                                      2.902112992431231|
|2021-01-01T00:01:00.000+08:00|                                     1.1755704705132448|
|2021-01-01T00:02:00.000+08:00|                                     -2.175570513757101|
|2021-01-01T00:03:00.000+08:00|                                    -1.9021130389094498|
|2021-01-01T00:04:00.000+08:00|                                     0.9999999925494194|
|2021-01-01T00:05:00.000+08:00|                                      1.902113046743454|
|2021-01-01T00:06:00.000+08:00|                                    0.17557053610884188|
|2021-01-01T00:07:00.000+08:00|                                    -1.1755704886020932|
|2021-01-01T00:08:00.000+08:00|                                    -0.9021130371347148|
|2021-01-01T00:09:00.000+08:00|                                  3.552713678800501E-16|
|2021-01-01T00:10:00.000+08:00|                                     0.9021130371347154|
|2021-01-01T00:11:00.000+08:00|                                     1.1755704886020932|
|2021-01-01T00:12:00.000+08:00|                                   -0.17557053610884144|
|2021-01-01T00:13:00.000+08:00|                                     -1.902113046743454|
|2021-01-01T00:14:00.000+08:00|                                    -0.9999999925494196|
|2021-01-01T00:15:00.000+08:00|                                     1.9021130389094498|
|2021-01-01T00:16:00.000+08:00|                                     2.1755705137571004|
|2021-01-01T00:17:00.000+08:00|                                    -1.1755704705132448|
|2021-01-01T00:18:00.000+08:00|                                     -2.902112992431231|
|2021-01-01T00:19:00.000+08:00|                                 -3.552713678800501E-16|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-8-lowpass"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--5.8-lowpass"></a>

### [5.8 LowPass](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_5-8-lowpass)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-39-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-40"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-39)

```sql
create function lowpass as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.frequency.UDTFLowPass'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-39-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-40"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-39)

This function performs low-pass filtering on the input series and extracts components below the cutoff frequency. The timestamps of input will be ignored and all data points will be regarded as equidistant.

**Name:** LOWPASS

**Input:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `wpass`: The normalized cutoff frequency which values (0,1). This parameter cannot be lacked.

**Output:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. It is the input after filtering. The length and timestamps of output are the same as the input.

**Note:** `NaN` in the input series will be ignored.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-39-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-40"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-39)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d1.s1|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|       2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:02.000+08:00|     -2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:03.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:04.000+08:00|            1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:05.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:06.000+08:00|      0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:07.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:08.000+08:00|      -0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:09.000+08:00|            0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:10.000+08:00|       0.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:11.000+08:00|      1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:12.000+08:00|     -0.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:13.000+08:00|     -1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:14.000+08:00|           -1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:15.000+08:00|      1.9021131|
|1970-01-01T08:00:16.000+08:00|      2.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:17.000+08:00|     -1.1755705|
|1970-01-01T08:00:18.000+08:00|      -2.902113|
|1970-01-01T08:00:19.000+08:00|            0.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select lowpass(s1,'wpass'='0.45') from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
|                         Time|lowpass(root.test.d1.s1, "wpass"="0.45")|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                      1.9021130073323922|
|1970-01-01T08:00:01.000+08:00|                      1.1755704705132448|
|1970-01-01T08:00:02.000+08:00|                     -1.1755705286582614|
|1970-01-01T08:00:03.000+08:00|                     -1.9021130389094498|
|1970-01-01T08:00:04.000+08:00|                    7.450580419288145E-9|
|1970-01-01T08:00:05.000+08:00|                       1.902113046743454|
|1970-01-01T08:00:06.000+08:00|                      1.1755705212076808|
|1970-01-01T08:00:07.000+08:00|                     -1.1755704886020932|
|1970-01-01T08:00:08.000+08:00|                     -1.9021130222335536|
|1970-01-01T08:00:09.000+08:00|                   3.552713678800501E-16|
|1970-01-01T08:00:10.000+08:00|                      1.9021130222335536|
|1970-01-01T08:00:11.000+08:00|                      1.1755704886020932|
|1970-01-01T08:00:12.000+08:00|                     -1.1755705212076801|
|1970-01-01T08:00:13.000+08:00|                      -1.902113046743454|
|1970-01-01T08:00:14.000+08:00|                    -7.45058112983088E-9|
|1970-01-01T08:00:15.000+08:00|                      1.9021130389094498|
|1970-01-01T08:00:16.000+08:00|                      1.1755705286582616|
|1970-01-01T08:00:17.000+08:00|                     -1.1755704705132448|
|1970-01-01T08:00:18.000+08:00|                     -1.9021130073323924|
|1970-01-01T08:00:19.000+08:00|                  -2.664535259100376E-16|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------+
```

Note: The input is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>y=sin(2\pi t/4)+2sin(2\pi t/5)</annotation></semantics></math>

y=sin(2πt/4)+2sin(2πt/5) with a length of 20. Thus, the output is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>/</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>y=2sin(2\pi t/5)</annotation></semantics></math>

y=2sin(2πt/5) after low-pass filtering.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-data-matching"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.-data-matching"></a>

## [6. Data Matching](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-data-matching)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-1-cov"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.1-cov"></a>

### [6.1 Cov](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-1-cov)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-40-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-41"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-40)

```sql
create function cov as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.dmatch.UDAFCov'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-40-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-41"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-40)

This function is used to calculate the population covariance.

**Name:** COV

**Input Series:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. There is only one data point in the series, whose timestamp is 0 and value is the population covariance.

**Note:**

- If a row contains missing points, null points or `NaN`, it will be ignored;
- If all rows are ignored, `NaN` will be output.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-40-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-41"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-40)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d2.s1|root.test.d2.s2|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|2020-01-01T00:00:02.000+08:00|          100.0|          101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:03.000+08:00|          101.0|           null|
|2020-01-01T00:00:04.000+08:00|          102.0|          101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:06.000+08:00|          104.0|          102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:08.000+08:00|          126.0|          102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:10.000+08:00|          108.0|          103.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:12.000+08:00|           null|          103.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:14.000+08:00|          112.0|          104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:15.000+08:00|          113.0|           null|
|2020-01-01T00:00:16.000+08:00|          114.0|          104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:18.000+08:00|          116.0|          105.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:20.000+08:00|          118.0|          105.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:22.000+08:00|          100.0|          106.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:26.000+08:00|          124.0|          108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:28.000+08:00|          126.0|          108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:30.000+08:00|            NaN|          108.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select cov(s1,s2) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|                         Time|cov(root.test.d2.s1, root.test.d2.s2)|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                   12.291666666666666|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-2-dtw"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.2-dtw"></a>

### [6.2 DTW](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-2-dtw)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-41-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-42"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-41)

```sql
create function dtw as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.dmatch.UDAFDtw'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-41-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-42"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-41)

This function is used to calculate the DTW distance between two input series.

**Name:** DTW

**Input Series:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. There is only one data point in the series, whose timestamp is 0 and value is the DTW distance.

**Note:**

- If a row contains missing points, null points or `NaN`, it will be ignored;
- If all rows are ignored, `0` will be output.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-41-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-42"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-41)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d2.s1|root.test.d2.s2|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.006+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.007+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.008+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.009+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.010+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.011+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.012+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.013+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.014+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.015+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.016+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.017+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.018+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.019+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.020+08:00|            1.0|            2.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select dtw(s1,s2) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|                         Time|dtw(root.test.d2.s1, root.test.d2.s2)|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                 20.0|
+-----------------------------+-------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-3-pearson"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.3-pearson"></a>

### [6.3 Pearson](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-3-pearson)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-42-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-43"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-42)

```sql
create function pearson as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.dmatch.UDAFPearson'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-42-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-43"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-42)

This function is used to calculate the Pearson Correlation Coefficient.

**Name:** PEARSON

**Input Series:** Only support two input series. The types are both INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. There is only one data point in the series, whose timestamp is 0 and value is the Pearson Correlation Coefficient.

**Note:**

- If a row contains missing points, null points or `NaN`, it will be ignored;
- If all rows are ignored, `NaN` will be output.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-42-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-43"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-42)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d2.s1|root.test.d2.s2|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
|2020-01-01T00:00:02.000+08:00|          100.0|          101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:03.000+08:00|          101.0|           null|
|2020-01-01T00:00:04.000+08:00|          102.0|          101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:06.000+08:00|          104.0|          102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:08.000+08:00|          126.0|          102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:10.000+08:00|          108.0|          103.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:12.000+08:00|           null|          103.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:14.000+08:00|          112.0|          104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:15.000+08:00|          113.0|           null|
|2020-01-01T00:00:16.000+08:00|          114.0|          104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:18.000+08:00|          116.0|          105.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:20.000+08:00|          118.0|          105.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:22.000+08:00|          100.0|          106.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:26.000+08:00|          124.0|          108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:28.000+08:00|          126.0|          108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:30.000+08:00|            NaN|          108.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select pearson(s1,s2) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
|                         Time|pearson(root.test.d2.s1, root.test.d2.s2)|
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                       0.5630881927754872|
+-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-4-ptnsym"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.4-ptnsym"></a>

### [6.4 PtnSym](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-4-ptnsym)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-43-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-44"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-43)

```sql
create function ptnsym as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.dmatch.UDTFPtnSym'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-43-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-44"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-43)

This function is used to find all symmetric subseries in the input whose degree of symmetry is less than the threshold. The degree of symmetry is calculated by DTW. The smaller the degree, the more symmetrical the series is.

**Name:** PATTERNSYMMETRIC

**Input Series:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE

**Parameter:**

- `window`: The length of the symmetric subseries. It's a positive integer and the default value is 10.
- `threshold`: The threshold of the degree of symmetry. It's non-negative. Only the subseries whose degree of symmetry is below it will be output. By default, all subseries will be output.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is DOUBLE. Each data point in the output series corresponds to a symmetric subseries. The output timestamp is the starting timestamp of the subseries and the output value is the degree of symmetry.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--example"></a>

#### [Example](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--example)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d1.s4|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|2021-01-01T12:00:00.000+08:00|            1.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:01.000+08:00|            2.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:02.000+08:00|            3.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:03.000+08:00|            2.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:04.000+08:00|            1.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:05.000+08:00|            1.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:06.000+08:00|            1.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:07.000+08:00|            1.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:08.000+08:00|            2.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:09.000+08:00|            3.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:10.000+08:00|            2.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:11.000+08:00|            1.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select ptnsym(s4, 'window'='5', 'threshold'='0') from root.test.d1
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+------------------------------------------------------+
|                         Time|ptnsym(root.test.d1.s4, "window"="5", "threshold"="0")|
+-----------------------------+------------------------------------------------------+
|2021-01-01T12:00:00.000+08:00|                                                   0.0|
|2021-01-01T12:00:07.000+08:00|                                                   0.0|
+-----------------------------+------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-5-xcorr"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.5-xcorr"></a>

### [6.5 XCorr](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-5-xcorr)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-44-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-45"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-44)

```sql
create function xcorr as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.dmatch.UDTFXCorr'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-44-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-45"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-44)

This function is used to calculate the cross correlation function of given two time series. For discrete time series, cross correlation is given by

<math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>N</mi></mfrac><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>N</mi></munderover><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>]</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>]</mo></mrow><annotation>CR(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=1}^N S_1[m]S_2[m+n] </annotation></semantics></math>

CR(n)=N1m=1∑NS1[m]S2[m+n]

which represent the similarities between two series with different index shifts.

**Name:** XCORR

**Input Series:** Only support two input numeric series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Output Series:** Output a single series with DOUBLE as datatype. There are

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>N</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><annotation>2N-1</annotation></semantics></math>

2N−1 data points in the series, the center of which represents the cross correlation calculated with pre-aligned series(that is

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><annotation>CR(0)</annotation></semantics></math>

CR(0) in the formula above), and the previous(or post) values represent those with shifting the latter series forward(or backward otherwise) until the two series are no longer overlapped(not included). In short, the values of output series are given by(index starts from 1)

<math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>O</mi><mi>S</mi><mo>[</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>(</mo><mo>−</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>N</mi></mfrac><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>i</mi></munderover><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>]</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>i</mi><mi>f</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>i</mi><mo>&lt;</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>N</mi></mrow><annotation>OS[i] = CR(-N+i) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=1}^{i} S_1[m]S_2[N-i+m],\ if\ i &lt;= N </annotation></semantics></math>

OS[i]=CR(−N+i)=N1m=1∑iS1[m]S2[N−i+m], if i<=N

<math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><semantics><mrow><mi>O</mi><mi>S</mi><mo>[</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>N</mi></mfrac><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>N</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></munderover><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>]</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>i</mi><mi>f</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>i</mi><mo>&gt;</mo><mi>N</mi></mrow><annotation>OS[i] = CR(i-N) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=1}^{2N-i} S_1[i-N+m]S_2[m],\ if\ i &gt; N </annotation></semantics></math>

OS[i]=CR(i−N)=N1m=1∑2N−iS1[i−N+m]S2[m], if i>N

**Note:**

- `null` and `NaN` values in the input series will be ignored and treated as 0.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-43-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-44"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-43)

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+ |                         Time|root.test.d1.s1|root.test.d1.s2| +-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+ |2020-01-01T00:00:01.000+08:00|           null|              6| |2020-01-01T00:00:02.000+08:00|              2|              7| |2020-01-01T00:00:03.000+08:00|              3|            NaN| |2020-01-01T00:00:04.000+08:00|              4|              9| |2020-01-01T00:00:05.000+08:00|              5|             10| +-----------------------------+---------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select xcorr(s1, s2) from root.test.d1 where time <= 2020-01-01 00:00:05
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------------------------------+ |                         Time|xcorr(root.test.d1.s1, root.test.d1.s2)| +-----------------------------+---------------------------------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.000+08:00|                                    0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|                                   10.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|                                   16.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|                                  16.75| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|                                   20.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|                                   13.2| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.006+08:00|                                    5.6| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.007+08:00|                                    7.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.008+08:00|                                    0.0| +-----------------------------+---------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-6-pattern-match"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--6.6-pattern_match"></a>

### [6.6 Pattern\_match](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_6-6-pattern-match)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-45-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-46"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-45)

```sql
create function pattern_match as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.match.UDAFPatternMatch'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-45-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-46"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-45)

This function performs pattern matching between an input time series and a predefined pattern. A match is considered successful if the similarity measure (distance) is less than or equal to a specified threshold. The results are output as a JSON list.

**Name**: PATTERN\_MATCH

**Input** **Series**: Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE/ BOOLEAN

**Parameter**:

- `timePattern` : A comma-separated string of timestamps (e.g., `"t1,t2,t3"`). Length must be **greater than 1**. Required.
- `valuePattern` : A comma-separated string of numerical values corresponding to `timePattern`. Length must **match** `timePattern` and be greater than 1. Required.

> For boolean values: Use `1` for `true` and `0` for `false`.

- `theshold`: Float-type similarity threshold. Required.

**Output** **Series**: A JSON list containing all successfully matched segments. Each entry includes: start timestamp `startTime`, end timestamp `endTime`, calculated similarity value `distance`.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--example-1"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--example-2"></a>

#### [Example](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--example-1)

1. Linear Data

Input series:

```sql
IoTDB> select s0 from root.** +-----------------------------+-------------+ |                         Time|root.db.d0.s0| +-----------------------------+-------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|          0.0| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|          1.1| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|          1.2| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|          1.3| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|          0.0| +-----------------------------+-------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select pattern_match (s0, "timePattern"="1,2,3", "valuePattern"="1.1,1.2,1.3", "threshold"="0.5") as match_result from root.db.d0
```

Output series:

```sql
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                      match_result|
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|[{"distance":0.200000,"startTime":1,"endTime":3}, {"distance":0.000000,"startTime":2,"endTime":4}]|
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

2. Boolean Data

Input series:

```sql
IoTDB> select s1 from root.** +-----------------------------+-------------+ |                         Time|root.db.d0.s1| +-----------------------------+-------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|        false| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|        false| +-----------------------------+-------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select pattern_match (s1, "timePattern"="1,2,3", "valuePattern"="1,1,1", "threshold"="0.5") as match_result from root.db.d0
```

Output series:

```sql
+-------------------------------------------------+
|                                     match_result|
+-------------------------------------------------+
|[{"distance":0.000000,"startTime":1,"endTime":3}]|
+-------------------------------------------------+
```

3. V-shaped Data

Input series:

```sql
IoTDB> select s2 from root.**
+-----------------------------+-------------+
|                         Time|root.db.d0.s2|
+-----------------------------+-------------+
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|          0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|         -1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|         -2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|         -3.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|         -2.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.006+08:00|         -1.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.007+08:00|         -0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.008+08:00|         -0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.009+08:00|         -0.0|
|1970-01-01T08:00:00.010+08:00|         -0.0|
+-----------------------------+-------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select pattern_match (s2, "timePattern"="1,2,3,4,5,6,7", "valuePattern"="0.0,-1.0,-2.0,-3.0,-2.0,-1.0,-0.0", "threshold"="10") as match_result from root.db.d0
```

Output series:

```sql
+----------------------------------------------+
|                                  match_result|
+----------------------------------------------+
|[{"distance":0.53,"startTime":1,"endTime":10}]|
+----------------------------------------------+
```

4. Multiple Matching Pattern

Input series:

```sql
IoTDB> select s0,s1 from root.** +-----------------------------+-------------+-------------+ |                         Time|root.db.d0.s0|root.db.d0.s1| +-----------------------------+-------------+-------------+ |1970-01-01T08:00:00.001+08:00|          0.0|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.002+08:00|          1.1|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.003+08:00|          1.2|         true| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.004+08:00|          1.3|        false| |1970-01-01T08:00:00.005+08:00|          0.0|        false| +-----------------------------+-------------+-------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select pattern_match (s0, "timePattern"="1,2,3", "valuePattern"="1.1,1.2,1.3", "threshold"="0.5") as match_result1, pattern_match (s1, "timePattern"="1,2,3", "valuePattern"="1,1,1",
 "threshold"="0.5") as match_result2 from root.db.d0
```

Output series:

```sql
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                     match_result1|                                    match_result2|
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
|[{"distance":0.200000,"startTime":1,"endTime":3}, {"distance":0.000000,"startTime":2,"endTime":4}]|[{"distance":0.000000,"startTime":1,"endTime":3}]|
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-data-repairing"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--7.-data-repairing"></a>

## [7. Data Repairing](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-data-repairing)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-1-timestamprepair"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--7.1-timestamprepair"></a>

### [7.1 TimestampRepair](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-1-timestamprepair)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-46-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-47"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-46)

```sql
create function timestamprepair as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.drepair.UDTFTimestampRepair'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-46-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-47"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-46)

This function is used for timestamp repair. According to the given standard time interval, the method of minimizing the repair cost is adopted. By fine-tuning the timestamps, the original data with unstable timestamp interval is repaired to strictly equispaced data. If no standard time interval is given, this function will use the **median**, **mode** or **cluster** of the time interval to estimate the standard time interval.

**Name:** TIMESTAMPREPAIR

**Input Series:** Only support a single input series. The data type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `interval`: The standard time interval whose unit is millisecond. It is a positive integer. By default, it will be estimated according to the given method.
- `method`: The method to estimate the standard time interval, which is 'median', 'mode' or 'cluster'. This parameter is only valid when `interval` is not given. By default, median will be used.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is the same as the input. This series is the input after repairing.

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-44-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-45"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-44)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--manually-specify-the-standard-time-interval"></a>

##### [Manually Specify the Standard Time Interval](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--manually-specify-the-standard-time-interval)

When `interval` is given, this function repairs according to the given standard time interval.

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+ |                         Time|root.test.d2.s1| +-----------------------------+---------------+ |2021-07-01T12:00:00.000+08:00|            1.0| |2021-07-01T12:00:10.000+08:00|            2.0| |2021-07-01T12:00:19.000+08:00|            3.0| |2021-07-01T12:00:30.000+08:00|            4.0| |2021-07-01T12:00:40.000+08:00|            5.0| |2021-07-01T12:00:50.000+08:00|            6.0| |2021-07-01T12:01:01.000+08:00|            7.0| |2021-07-01T12:01:11.000+08:00|            8.0| |2021-07-01T12:01:21.000+08:00|            9.0| |2021-07-01T12:01:31.000+08:00|           10.0| +-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select timestamprepair(s1,'interval'='10000') from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------------------+
|                         Time|timestamprepair(root.test.d2.s1, "interval"="10000")|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------------------+
|2021-07-01T12:00:00.000+08:00|                                                 1.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:10.000+08:00|                                                 2.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:20.000+08:00|                                                 3.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:30.000+08:00|                                                 4.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:40.000+08:00|                                                 5.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:50.000+08:00|                                                 6.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:00.000+08:00|                                                 7.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:10.000+08:00|                                                 8.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:20.000+08:00|                                                 9.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:30.000+08:00|                                                10.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:40.000+08:00|                                                 NaN|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--automatically-estimate-the-standard-time-interval"></a>

##### [Automatically Estimate the Standard Time Interval](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--automatically-estimate-the-standard-time-interval)

When `interval` is default, this function estimates the standard time interval.

Input series is the same as above, the SQL for query is shown below:

```sql
select timestamprepair(s1) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------+
|                         Time|timestamprepair(root.test.d2.s1)|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------+
|2021-07-01T12:00:00.000+08:00|                             1.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:10.000+08:00|                             2.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:20.000+08:00|                             3.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:30.000+08:00|                             4.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:40.000+08:00|                             5.0|
|2021-07-01T12:00:50.000+08:00|                             6.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:00.000+08:00|                             7.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:10.000+08:00|                             8.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:20.000+08:00|                             9.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:30.000+08:00|                            10.0|
|2021-07-01T12:01:40.000+08:00|                             NaN|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-2-valuefill"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--7.2-valuefill"></a>

### [7.2 ValueFill](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--_7-2-valuefill)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-47-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-48"></a>

#### [Registration statement](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--registration-statement-47)

```sql
create function valuefill as 'org.apache.iotdb.library.drepair.UDTFValueFill'
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-47-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-48"></a>

#### [Usage](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--usage-47)

This function is used to impute time series. Several methods are supported.

**Name**: ValueFill **Input Series:** Only support a single input series. The type is INT32 / INT64 / FLOAT / DOUBLE.

**Parameters:**

- `method`: {"mean", "previous", "linear", "likelihood", "AR", "MA", "SCREEN"}, default "linear".
  Method to use for imputation in series. "mean": use global mean value to fill holes; "previous": propagate last valid observation forward to next valid. "linear": simplest interpolation method; "likelihood":Maximum likelihood estimation based on the normal distribution of speed; "AR": auto regression; "MA": moving average; "SCREEN": speed constraint.

**Output Series:** Output a single series. The type is the same as the input. This series is the input after repairing.

**Note:** AR method use AR(1) model. Input value should be auto-correlated, or the function would output a single point (0, 0.0).

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-45-2"></a>
<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-46"></a>

#### [Examples](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--examples-45)

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--fill-with-linear"></a>

##### [Fill with linear](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--fill-with-linear)

When `method` is "linear" or the default, Screen method is used to impute.

Input series:

```
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|                         Time|root.test.d2.s1|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
|2020-01-01T00:00:02.000+08:00|            NaN|
|2020-01-01T00:00:03.000+08:00|          101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:04.000+08:00|          102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:06.000+08:00|          104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:08.000+08:00|          126.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:10.000+08:00|          108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:14.000+08:00|            NaN|
|2020-01-01T00:00:15.000+08:00|          113.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:16.000+08:00|          114.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:18.000+08:00|          116.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:20.000+08:00|            NaN|
|2020-01-01T00:00:22.000+08:00|            NaN|
|2020-01-01T00:00:26.000+08:00|          124.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:28.000+08:00|          126.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:30.000+08:00|          128.0|
+-----------------------------+---------------+
```

SQL for query:

```sql
select valuefill(s1) from root.test.d2
```

Output series:

```
+-----------------------------+--------------------------+
|                         Time|valuefill(root.test.d2.s1)|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------+
|2020-01-01T00:00:02.000+08:00|                     101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:03.000+08:00|                     101.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:04.000+08:00|                     102.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:06.000+08:00|                     104.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:08.000+08:00|                     126.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:10.000+08:00|                     108.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:14.000+08:00|                     110.5|
|2020-01-01T00:00:15.000+08:00|                     113.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:16.000+08:00|                     114.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:18.000+08:00|                     116.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:20.000+08:00|        118.66666666666667|
|2020-01-01T00:00:22.000+08:00|        121.33333333333333|
|2020-01-01T00:00:26.000+08:00|                     124.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:28.000+08:00|                     126.0|
|2020-01-01T00:00:30.000+08:00|                     128.0|
+-----------------------------+--------------------------+
```

<a id="sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--previous-fill"></a>

##### [Previous Fill](#sql-manual-udf-libraries_apache--previous-fill)

When `method` is "previous", previous method is used.

Input series is the same as above, the SQL for query is shown below:

```sql
select valuefill(s1,"method"="previous") from root.test.d2
```
