# Docker Deployment

[Skip to main content](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--main-content)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--docker-deployment"></a>

# Docker Deployment

8/9/24About 6 min

---

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--docker-deployment-2"></a>

# [Docker Deployment](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--docker-deployment)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-environmental-preparation"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--1.-environmental-preparation"></a>

## [1. Environmental Preparation](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-environmental-preparation)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-1-docker-installation"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--1.1-docker-installation"></a>

### [1.1 Docker Installation](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-1-docker-installation)

```sql
#Taking Ubuntu as an example, other operating systems can search for installation methods themselves
#step1: Install some necessary system tools
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get -y install apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl software-properties-common
#step2: Install GPG certificate
curl -fsSL https://mirrors.aliyun.com/docker-ce/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo apt-key add -
#step3: Write software source information
sudo add-apt-repository "deb [arch=amd64] https://mirrors.aliyun.com/docker-ce/linux/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs) stable"
#step4: Update and install Docker CE
sudo apt-get -y update
sudo apt-get -y install docker-ce
#step5: Set Docker to start automatically upon startup
sudo systemctl enable docker
#step6： Verify if Docker installation is successful
docker --version  #Display version information, indicating successful installation
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-2-docker-compose-installation"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--1.2-docker-compose-installation"></a>

### [1.2 Docker-compose Installation](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_1-2-docker-compose-installation)

```sql
#Installation command
curl -L "https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v2.20.0/docker-compose-$(uname -s)-$(uname -m)" -o /usr/local/bin/docker-compose
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/docker-compose
ln -s  /usr/local/bin/docker-compose  /usr/bin/docker-compose
#Verify if the installation was successful
docker-compose --version  #Displaying version information indicates successful installation
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-stand-alone-deployment"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.-stand-alone-deployment"></a>

## [2. Stand-Alone Deployment](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-stand-alone-deployment)

This section demonstrates how to deploy a standalone Docker version of 1C1D.

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-1-pull-image-file"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.1-pull-image-file"></a>

### [2.1 Pull Image File](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-1-pull-image-file)

The Docker image of Apache IoTDB has been uploaded to <https://hub.docker.com/r/apache/iotdb>.

Taking obtaining version 2.0.x as an example, pull the image command:

```bash
docker pull apache/iotdb:2.0.x-standalone
```

View image:

```bash
docker images
```

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e6-8b-89-e5-8f-96-e9-95-9c-e5-83-8f_d827aecf3765c84c.png)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-2-create-docker-bridge-network"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.2-create-docker-bridge-network"></a>

### [2.2 Create Docker Bridge Network](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-2-create-docker-bridge-network)

```bash
docker network create --driver=bridge --subnet=172.18.0.0/16 --gateway=172.18.0.1  iotdb
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose"></a>

### [2.3 Write The Yml File For Docker-Compose](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose)

Here we take the example of consolidating the IoTDB installation directory and yml files in the/docker iotdb folder:

The file directory structure is:`/docker-iotdb/iotdb`, `/docker-iotdb/docker-compose-standalone.yml`

```bash
docker-iotdb：
├── iotdb  #Iotdb installation directory
│── docker-compose-standalone.yml #YML file for standalone Docker Composer
```

The complete docker-compose-standalone.yml content is as follows:

```bash
version: "3"
services:
  iotdb-service:
    image: apache/iotdb:latest #The image used
    hostname: iotdb
    container_name: iotdb
    restart: always       
    ports:
      - "6667:6667"
    environment:
      - cn_internal_address=iotdb
      - cn_internal_port=10710
      - cn_consensus_port=10720
      - cn_seed_config_node=iotdb:10710
      - dn_rpc_address=iotdb
      - dn_internal_address=iotdb
      - dn_rpc_port=6667
      - dn_internal_port=10730
      - dn_mpp_data_exchange_port=10740
      - dn_schema_region_consensus_port=10750
      - dn_data_region_consensus_port=10760
      - dn_seed_config_node=iotdb:10710
    privileged: true
    volumes:
        - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
        - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs
    networks:
      iotdb:
        ipv4_address: 172.18.0.6
    # Note: Some environments set an extremely high container nofile limit (~2^30 = 1073741824).
    # This can make the startup step "Checking whether the ports are already occupied..." appear to hang (lsof slow).
    # If you see that line for a long time, lower the nofile limit by uncommenting below:
    # ulimits:
    #   nofile:
    #     soft: 1048576
# hard: 1048576 networks:iotdb:external: true
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-4-start-iotdb"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.4-start-iotdb"></a>

### [2.4 Start IoTDB](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-4-start-iotdb)

Use the following command to start:

```bash
cd　/docker-iotdb
docker-compose -f docker-compose-standalone.yml up  -d  #Background startup
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-5-alidate-deployment"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.5-alidate-deployment"></a>

### [2.5 alidate Deployment](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-5-alidate-deployment)

- Viewing the log, the following words indicate successful startup

```sql
docker logs -f iotdb-datanode #View log command
2024-07-21 08:22:38,457 [main] INFO  o.a.i.db.service.DataNode:227 - Congratulations, IoTDB DataNode is set up successfully. Now, enjoy yourself!
```

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-aa-8c-e8-af-81-e9-83-a8-e7-bd-b2_d841a72765d7926e.png)

- Enter the container to view the service running status and activation information

View the launched container

```sql
docker ps
```

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-aa-8c-e8-af-81-e9-83-a8-e7-bd-b22_7c52180e32f6161c.png)

Enter the container, log in to the database through CLI, and use the `show cluster` command to view the service status and activation status

```sql
docker exec -it iotdb   /bin/bash        #Entering the container
./start-cli.sh -h iotdb                  #Log in to the database
IoTDB> show cluster                      #View status
```

You can see that all services are running and the activation status shows as activated.

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-aa-8c-e8-af-81-e9-83-a8-e7-bd-b23_52c0b4be1e1977af.png)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-6-map-conf-directory-optional"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--2.6-map-conf-directory-optional"></a>

### [2.6 Map/conf Directory (optional)](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_2-6-map-conf-directory-optional)

If you want to directly modify the configuration file in the physical machine in the future, you can map the/conf folder in the container in three steps:

Step 1: Copy the /conf directory from the container to `/docker-iotdb/iotdb/conf`

```bash
docker cp iotdb:/iotdb/conf /docker-iotdb/iotdb/conf
```

Step 2: Add mappings in docker-compose-standalone.yml

```bash
    volumes:
        - ./iotdb/conf:/iotdb/conf   #Add mapping for this/conf folder
        - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
        - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs
```

Step 3: Restart IoTDB

```bash
docker-compose  -f docker-compose-standalone.yml  up  -d
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-cluster-deployment"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.-cluster-deployment"></a>

## [3. Cluster Deployment](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-cluster-deployment)

This section describes how to manually deploy an instance that includes 3 Config Nodes and 3 Data Nodes, commonly known as a 3C3D cluster.

![](assets/images/20240705141552_06cb0b40e25be32b.png)

**Note: The cluster version currently only supports host and overlay networks, and does not support bridge networks.**

Taking the host network as an example, we will demonstrate how to deploy a 3C3D cluster.

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-1-set-host-name"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.1-set-host-name"></a>

### [3.1 Set Host Name](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-1-set-host-name)

Assuming there are currently three Linux servers, the IP addresses and service role assignments are as follows:

| Node IP | Host Name | Service |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 192.168.1.3 | iotdb-1 | ConfigNode、DataNode |
| 192.168.1.4 | iotdb-2 | ConfigNode、DataNode |
| 192.168.1.5 | iotdb-3 | ConfigNode、DataNode |

Configure the host names on three machines separately. To set the host names, configure `/etc/hosts` on the target server using the following command:

```bash
echo "192.168.1.3  iotdb-1"  >> /etc/hosts
echo "192.168.1.4  iotdb-2"  >> /etc/hosts
echo "192.168.1.5  iotdb-3"  >> /etc/hosts
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-2-pull-image-file"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.2-pull-image-file"></a>

### [3.2 Pull Image File](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-2-pull-image-file)

The Docker image of Apache IoTDB has been uploaded tohttps://hub.docker.com/r/apache/iotdb。

Pull IoTDB images from three servers separately, taking version 2.0.x as an example. The pull image command is:

```sql
docker pull apache/iotdb:2.0.x-standalone
```

View image:

```sql
docker images
```

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-9b-86-e7-be-a4-e7-89-881_8944180926f6f301.png)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose"></a>

### [3.3 Write The Yml File For Docker Compose](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-3-write-the-yml-file-for-docker-compose)

Here we take the example of consolidating the IoTDB installation directory and yml files in the `/docker-iotdb` folder:

The file directory structure is :`/docker-iotdb/iotdb`, `/docker-iotdb/confignode.yml`，`/docker-iotdb/datanode.yml`

```sql
docker-iotdb：
├── confignode.yml #Yml file of confignode
├── datanode.yml   #Yml file of datanode
└── iotdb          #IoTDB installation directory
```

On each server, two yml files need to be written, namely confignnode. yml and datanode. yml. The example of yml is as follows:

**confignode.yml：**

```bash
#confignode.yml
version: "3"
services:
  iotdb-confignode:
    image: iotdb-enterprise:2.0.x-standalone #The image used
    hostname: iotdb-1|iotdb-2|iotdb-3 #Choose from three options based on the actual situation
    container_name: iotdb-confignode
    command: ["bash", "-c", "entrypoint.sh confignode"]
    restart: always
    environment:
      - cn_internal_address=iotdb-1|iotdb-2|iotdb-3 #Choose from three options based on the actual situation
      - cn_internal_port=10710
      - cn_consensus_port=10720
      - cn_seed_config_node=iotdb-1:10710   #The default first node is the seed node
      - schema_replication_factor=3         #Number of metadata copies
      - data_replication_factor=2           #Number of data replicas
    privileged: true
    volumes:
      - ./iotdb/activation:/iotdb/activation
      - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
      - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs
      - /usr/sbin/dmidecode:/usr/sbin/dmidecode:ro
    network_mode: "host"    #Using the host network
    # Note: Some environments set an extremely high container nofile limit (~2^30 = 1073741824).
    # This can make the startup step "Checking whether the ports are already occupied..." appear to hang (lsof slow).
    # If you see that line for a long time, lower the nofile limit by uncommenting below:
    # ulimits:
    #   nofile:
    #     soft: 1048576
    #     hard: 1048576
```

**datanode.yml：**

```bash
#datanode.yml
version: "3"
services:
  iotdb-datanode:
    image: iotdb-enterprise:2.0.x-standalone #The image used
    hostname: iotdb-1|iotdb-2|iotdb-3 #Choose from three options based on the actual situation
    container_name: iotdb-datanode
    command: ["bash", "-c", "entrypoint.sh datanode"]
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "6667:6667"
    privileged: true
    environment:
      - dn_rpc_address=iotdb-1|iotdb-2|iotdb-3 #Choose from three options based on the actual situation
      - dn_internal_address=iotdb-1|iotdb-2|iotdb-3 #Choose from three options based on the actual situation
      - dn_seed_config_node=iotdb-1:10710      #The default first node is the seed node
      - dn_rpc_port=6667
      - dn_internal_port=10730
      - dn_mpp_data_exchange_port=10740
      - dn_schema_region_consensus_port=10750
      - dn_data_region_consensus_port=10760
      - schema_replication_factor=3         #Number of metadata copies
      - data_replication_factor=2           #Number of data replicas
    volumes:
      - ./iotdb/activation:/iotdb/activation
      - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
      - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs
      - /usr/sbin/dmidecode:/usr/sbin/dmidecode:ro
    network_mode: "host"   #Using the host network
    # Note: Some environments set an extremely high container nofile limit (~2^30 = 1073741824).
    # This can make the startup step "Checking whether the ports are already occupied..." appear to hang (lsof slow).
    # If you see that line for a long time, lower the nofile limit by uncommenting below:
    # ulimits:
    #   nofile:
    #     soft: 1048576
    #     hard: 1048576
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-4-starting-confignode-for-the-first-time"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.4-starting-confignode-for-the-first-time"></a>

### [3.4 Starting Confignode For The First Time](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-4-starting-confignode-for-the-first-time)

First, start configNodes on each of the three servers to obtain the machine code. Pay attention to the startup order, start the first iotdb-1 first, then start iotdb-2 and iotdb-3.

```bash
cd　/docker-iotdb
docker-compose -f confignode.yml up  -d #Background startup
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-5-start-datanode"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.5-start-datanode"></a>

### [3.5 Start Datanode](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-5-start-datanode)

Start datanodes on 3 servers separately

```sql
cd /docker-iotdb
docker-compose  -f  datanode.yml  up -d #Background startup
```

![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-9b-86-e7-be-a4-e7-89-882_b03c702b3e29f347.png)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-6-validate-deployment"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.6-validate-deployment"></a>

### [3.6 Validate Deployment](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-6-validate-deployment)

- Viewing the logs, the following words indicate that the datanode has successfully started


```sql
docker logs -f iotdb-datanode #View log command
2024-07-21 09:40:58,120 [main] INFO  o.a.i.db.service.DataNode:227 - Congratulations, IoTDB DataNode is set up successfully. Now, enjoy yourself!
```

  ![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-9b-86-e7-be-a4-e7-89-883_e79875c8bf4d68b5.png)
- Enter any container to view the service running status and activation information

  View the launched container


```sql
docker ps
```

  ![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-9b-86-e7-be-a4-e7-89-884_6aa54ca447f52052.png)

  Enter the container, log in to the database through CLI, and use the `show cluster` command to view the service status and activation status


```sql
docker exec -it iotdb-datanode /bin/bash #Entering the container
./start-cli.sh -h iotdb-1                #Log in to the database
IoTDB> show cluster                      #View status
```

  You can see that all services are running and the activation status shows as activated.

  ![](assets/images/e5-bc-80-e6-ba-90-e9-9b-86-e7-be-a4-e7-89-885_6867a8e7b9c781f6.png)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-7-map-conf-directory-optional"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--3.7-map-conf-directory-optional"></a>

### [3.7 Map/conf Directory (optional)](#deployment-and-maintenance-docker-deployment_apache--_3-7-map-conf-directory-optional)

If you want to directly modify the configuration file in the physical machine in the future, you can map the/conf folder in the container in three steps:

Step 1: Copy the `/conf` directory from the container to `/docker-iotdb/iotdb/conf` on each of the three servers

```bash
docker cp iotdb-confignode:/iotdb/conf /docker-iotdb/iotdb/conf
or
docker cp iotdb-datanode:/iotdb/conf   /docker-iotdb/iotdb/conf
```

Step 2: Add `/conf` directory mapping in `confignode.yml` and `datanode. yml` on 3 servers

```bash
#confignode.yml
    volumes:
      - ./iotdb/conf:/iotdb/conf  #Add mapping for this /conf folder
      - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
      - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs

#datanode.yml
    volumes:
      - ./iotdb/conf:/iotdb/conf   #Add mapping for this /conf folder
      - ./iotdb/data:/iotdb/data
      - ./iotdb/logs:/iotdb/logs
```

Step 3: Restart IoTDB on 3 servers

```bash
cd /docker-iotdb
docker-compose  -f confignode.yml  up  -d
docker-compose  -f datanode.yml    up  -d
```

---

<!-- page_index: 46 -->

# Kubernetes

[Skip to main content](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--main-content)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--kubernetes"></a>

# Kubernetes

1/12/25About 5 min

---

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--kubernetes-2"></a>

# [Kubernetes](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--kubernetes)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_1-environment-preparation"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--1.-environment-preparation"></a>

## [1. Environment Preparation](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_1-environment-preparation)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_1-1-prepare-a-kubernetes-cluster"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--1.1-prepare-a-kubernetes-cluster"></a>

### [1.1 Prepare a Kubernetes Cluster](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_1-1-prepare-a-kubernetes-cluster)

Ensure that you have an available Kubernetes cluster (minimum recommended version: Kubernetes 1.24) as the foundation for deploying the IoTDB cluster.

Kubernetes Version Requirement: The recommended version is Kubernetes 1.24 or above.

IoTDB Version Requirement: The version of IoTDB must not be lower than v1.3.3.

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-create-namespace"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--2.-create-namespace"></a>

## [2. Create Namespace](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-create-namespace)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-1-create-namespace"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--2.1-create-namespace"></a>

### [2.1 Create Namespace](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-1-create-namespace)

> Note: Before executing the namespace creation operation, verify that the specified namespace name has not been used in the Kubernetes cluster. If the namespace already exists, the creation command will fail, which may lead to errors during the deployment process.

```bash
kubectl create ns iotdb-ns
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-2-view-namespace"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--2.2-view-namespace"></a>

### [2.2 View Namespace](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_2-2-view-namespace)

```bash
kubectl get ns
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-create-persistentvolume-pv"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--3.-create-persistentvolume-pv"></a>

## [3. Create PersistentVolume (PV)](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-create-persistentvolume-pv)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-1-create-pv-configuration-file"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--3.1-create-pv-configuration-file"></a>

### [3.1 Create PV Configuration File](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-1-create-pv-configuration-file)

PV is used for persistent storage of IoTDB's ConfigNode and DataNode data. You need to create one PV for each node.

> Note: One ConfigNode and one DataNode count as two nodes, requiring two PVs.

For example, with 3 ConfigNodes and 3 DataNodes:

1. Create a `pv.yaml` file and make six copies, renaming them to `pv01.yaml` through `pv06.yaml`.

```bash
# Create a directory to store YAML files
# Create pv.yaml file touch pv.yaml
```

2. Modify the `name` and `path` in each file to ensure consistency.

**pv.yaml Example：**

```yaml
# pv.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: iotdb-pv-01
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi # Storage capacity
  accessModes: # Access modes
    - ReadWriteOnce
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain # Reclaim policy
  # Storage class name, if using local static storage, do not configure; if using dynamic storage, this must be set
  storageClassName: local-storage 
  # Add the corresponding configuration based on your storage type
  hostPath: # If using a local path
    path: /data/k8s-data/iotdb-pv-01
    type: DirectoryOrCreate  # If this line is not configured, you need to manually create the directory
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-2-apply-pv-configuration"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--3.2-apply-pv-configuration"></a>

### [3.2 Apply PV Configuration](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-2-apply-pv-configuration)

```bash
kubectl apply -f pv01.yaml
kubectl apply -f pv-02.yaml
...
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-3-view-pv"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--3.3-view-pv"></a>

### [3.3 View PV](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-3-view-pv)

```bash
kubectl get pv
```

![](assets/images/kubernetes01_5e29b0a9b346973d.png)<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-4-manually-create-directories"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--3.4-manually-create-directories"></a>

### [3.4 Manually Create Directories](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_3-4-manually-create-directories)

> Note: If the type in the hostPath of the YAML file is not configured, you need to manually create the corresponding directories.

Create the corresponding directories on all Kubernetes nodes:

```bash
mkdir -p /data/k8s-data/iotdb-pv-01
mkdir -p /data/k8s-data/iotdb-pv-02
...
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_4-install-helm"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--4.-install-helm"></a>

## [4. Install Helm](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_4-install-helm)

For installation steps, please refer to the[Helm Official Website.](https://helm.sh/zh/docs/intro/install/)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-configure-iotdb-helm-chart"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--5.-configure-iotdb-helm-chart"></a>

## [5. Configure IoTDB Helm Chart](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-configure-iotdb-helm-chart)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-1-clone-iotdb-kubernetes-deployment-code"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--5.1-clone-iotdb-kubernetes-deployment-code"></a>

### [5.1 Clone IoTDB Kubernetes Deployment Code](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-1-clone-iotdb-kubernetes-deployment-code)

Clone Helm : [Source Code](https://github.com/apache/iotdb-extras/tree/master/helm)

If encountering proxy issues, cancel proxy settings:

> The git clone error is as follows, indicating that the proxy has been configured and needs to be turned off fatal: unable to access '<https://github.com/apache/iotdb-extras.git>': gnutls\_handshake() failed: The TLS connection was non-properly terminated.

```bash
unset HTTPS_PROXY
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-2-modify-yaml-files"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--5.2-modify-yaml-files"></a>

### [5.2 Modify YAML Files](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_5-2-modify-yaml-files)

> Ensure that the version used is supported (>=1.3.3.2):

**values.yaml Example：**

```yaml
nameOverride: "iotdb"
fullnameOverride: "iotdb"   # Name after installation

image:
  repository: apache/iotdb
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent
  tag: latest    # Repository and version used

storage:
  # Storage class name, if using local static storage, do not configure; if using dynamic storage, this must be set
  className: local-storage

datanode:
  name: datanode
  nodeCount: 3        # Number of DataNode nodes
  enableRestService: true
  storageCapacity: 10Gi       # Available space for DataNode
  resources:
    requests:
      memory: 2Gi    # Initial memory size for DataNode
      cpu: 1000m     # Initial CPU size for DataNode
    limits:
      memory: 4Gi    # Maximum memory size for DataNode
      cpu: 1000m     # Maximum CPU size for DataNode

confignode:
  name: confignode
  nodeCount: 3      # Number of ConfigNode nodes
  storageCapacity: 10Gi      # Available space for ConfigNode
  resources:
    requests:
      memory: 512Mi    # Initial memory size for ConfigNode
      cpu: 1000m      # Initial CPU size for ConfigNode
    limits:
      memory: 1024Mi   # Maximum memory size for ConfigNode
      cpu: 2000m     # Maximum CPU size for ConfigNode
  configNodeConsensusProtocolClass: org.apache.iotdb.consensus.ratis.RatisConsensus
  schemaReplicationFactor: 3
  schemaRegionConsensusProtocolClass: org.apache.iotdb.consensus.ratis.RatisConsensus
  dataReplicationFactor: 2
  dataRegionConsensusProtocolClass: org.apache.iotdb.consensus.iot.IoTConsensus
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-install-iotdb"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--6.-install-iotdb"></a>

## [6. Install IoTDB](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-install-iotdb)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-1-install-iotdb"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--6.1-install-iotdb"></a>

### [6.1 Install IoTDB](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-1-install-iotdb)

```bash
# Enter the directory cd iotdb-cluster-k8s/helm

# Install IoTDB helm install iotdb ./ -n iotdb-ns
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-2-view-helm-installation-list"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--6.2-view-helm-installation-list"></a>

### [6.2 View Helm Installation List](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-2-view-helm-installation-list)

```bash
# helm list helm list -n iotdb-ns
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-3-view-pods"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--6.3-view-pods"></a>

### [6.3 View Pods](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-3-view-pods)

```bash
# View IoTDB pods kubectl get pods -n iotdb-ns -o wide
```

After executing the command, if the output shows 6 Pods with confignode and datanode labels (3 each), it indicates a successful installation. Note that not all Pods may be in the Running state initially; inactive datanode Pods may keep restarting but will normalize after activation.

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-4-troubleshooting"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--6.4-troubleshooting"></a>

### [6.4 Troubleshooting](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_6-4-troubleshooting)

```bash
# View k8s creation logs kubectl get events -n iotdb-ns watch kubectl get events -n iotdb-ns

# Get detailed information kubectl describe pod confignode-0 -n iotdb-ns kubectl describe pod datanode-0 -n iotdb-ns

# View ConfigNode logs kubectl logs -n iotdb-ns confignode-0 -f
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-verify-iotdb"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--7.-verify-iotdb"></a>

## [7. Verify IoTDB](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-verify-iotdb)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-1-check-the-status-of-pods-within-the-namespace"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--7.1-check-the-status-of-pods-within-the-namespace"></a>

### [7.1 Check the Status of Pods within the Namespace](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-1-check-the-status-of-pods-within-the-namespace)

View the IP, status, and other information of the pods in the iotdb-ns namespace to ensure they are all running normally.

```bash
kubectl get pods -n iotdb-ns -o wide

# Example output:
# NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS         AGE   IP             NODE   NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
# confignode-0   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.187.14   a87    <none>           <none>
# confignode-1   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.191.75   a88    <none>           <none>
# confignode-2   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.187.16   a87    <none>           <none>
# datanode-0     1/1     Running   10 (5m54s ago)   75m   10.20.191.74   a88    <none>           <none>
# datanode-1     1/1     Running   10 (5m42s ago)   75m   10.20.187.15   a87    <none>           <none>
# datanode-2     1/1     Running   10 (5m55s ago)   75m   10.20.191.76   a88    <none>           <none>
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-2-check-the-port-mapping-within-the-namespace"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--7.2-check-the-port-mapping-within-the-namespace"></a>

### [7.2 Check the Port Mapping within the Namespace](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-2-check-the-port-mapping-within-the-namespace)

```bash
kubectl get svc -n iotdb-ns

# Example output:
# NAME             TYPE           CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)          AGE
# confignode-svc   NodePort       10.10.226.151   <none>        80:31026/TCP     7d8h
# datanode-svc     NodePort       10.10.194.225   <none>        6667:31563/TCP   7d8h
# jdbc-balancer    LoadBalancer   10.10.191.209   <pending>     6667:31895/TCP   7d8h
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-3-start-the-cli-script-on-any-server-to-verify-the-iotdb-cluster-status"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--7.3-start-the-cli-script-on-any-server-to-verify-the-iotdb-cluster-status"></a>

### [7.3 Start the CLI Script on Any Server to Verify the IoTDB Cluster Status](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_7-3-start-the-cli-script-on-any-server-to-verify-the-iotdb-cluster-status)

Use the port of jdbc-balancer and the IP of any k8s node.

```bash
start-cli.sh -h 172.20.31.86 -p 31895
start-cli.sh -h 172.20.31.87 -p 31895
start-cli.sh -h 172.20.31.88 -p 31895
```

![](assets/images/kubernetes02_5f73e327825dc4f0.png)<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-scaling"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--8.-scaling"></a>

## [8. Scaling](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-scaling)

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-1-add-new-pv"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--8.1-add-new-pv"></a>

### [8.1 Add New PV](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-1-add-new-pv)

Add a new PV; scaling is only possible with available PVs.

![](assets/images/kubernetes03_1bc9ee85bdae8b60.png)

**Note: DataNode cannot join the cluster after restart**

**Reason**：The static storage hostPath mode is configured, and the script modifies the `iotdb-system.properties` file to set `dn_data_dirs` to `/iotdb6/iotdb_data,/iotdb7/iotdb_data`. However, the default storage path `/iotdb/data` is not mounted, leading to data loss upon restart.
**Solution**：Mount the `/iotdb/data` directory as well, and ensure this setting is applied to both ConfigNode and DataNode to maintain data integrity and cluster stability.

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-2-scale-confignode"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--8.2-scale-confignode"></a>

### [8.2 Scale ConfigNode](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-2-scale-confignode)

Example: Scale from 3 ConfigNodes to 4 ConfigNodes

Modify the values.yaml file in iotdb-cluster-k8s/helm to change the number of ConfigNodes from 3 to 4.

```shell
helm upgrade iotdb . -n iotdb-ns
```

![](assets/images/kubernetes04_da25010cc2eaa8ad.png)<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-3-scale-datanode"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--8.3-scale-datanode"></a>

### [8.3 Scale DataNode](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-3-scale-datanode)

Example: Scale from 3 DataNodes to 4 DataNodes

Modify the values.yaml file in iotdb-cluster-k8s/helm to change the number of DataNodes from 3 to 4.

```shell
helm upgrade iotdb . -n iotdb-ns
```

<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-4-verify-iotdb-status"></a>
<a id="deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--8.4-verify-iotdb-status"></a>

### [8.4 Verify IoTDB Status](#deployment-and-maintenance-kubernetes_apache--_8-4-verify-iotdb-status)

```shell
kubectl get pods -n iotdb-ns -o wide

# NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS         AGE   IP             NODE   NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
# confignode-0   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.187.14   a87    <none>           <none>
# confignode-1   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.191.75   a88    <none>           <none>
# confignode-2   1/1     Running   0                75m   10.20.187.16   a87    <none>           <none>
# datanode-0     1/1     Running   10 (5m54s ago)   75m   10.20.191.74   a88    <none>           <none>
# datanode-1     1/1     Running   10 (5m42s ago)   75m   10.20.187.15   a87    <none>           <none>
# datanode-2     1/1     Running   10 (5m55s ago)   75m   10.20.191.76   a88    <none>           <none>
# datanode-3     1/1     Running   10 (5m55s ago)   75m   10.20.191.76   a88    <none>           <none>
```
